Name: spectral_stability_latency_optimization
Title: Spectral Stability-Guided Latency Optimization for Training-Free LLM Inference
Short Hypothesis: The spectral stability of hidden state activations in LLMs can serve as a proxy for computational difficulty, enabling dynamic resource allocation and latency-aware inference without retraining.
Related Work: Existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Geometric Signal Separation focus on activation geometry for robustness but do not explicitly optimize for latency. Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation addresses latency but lacks a formal spectral stability framework. Our proposal uniquely bridges spectral stability analysis with latency-aware inference, providing a theoretically grounded and computationally efficient solution.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) face significant challenges in real-world deployment due to unpredictable inference latency. We propose a training-free framework that leverages the spectral stability of hidden state activations to dynamically allocate computational resources, enforcing strict latency bounds. By performing on-the-fly singular value decomposition (SVD) on activations, we compute the spectral condition number and decay rate, which serve as proxies for computational difficulty. Tokens with high spectral stability are routed through fewer layers or lower-precision computations, while unstable tokens receive additional resources. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral stability to generalization under resource constraints. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic baselines, while maintaining deterministic latency guarantees. This work bridges foundational optimization theory with practical inference efficiency, offering a reproducible pathway for scalable LLM deployment.
Experiments: Evaluate spectral stability metrics (condition number, spectral decay rate) on activations from LLMs (e.g., GPT-3, LLaMA) across diverse tasks (mathematical reasoning, code generation).
Implement dynamic resource allocation based on spectral stability, including layer skipping, precision switching, and attention head pruning.
Benchmark against static and heuristic latency optimization baselines (e.g., fixed layer skipping, uniform precision reduction) on single-GPU setups.
Measure throughput, accuracy, and latency variance under controlled distributional shifts (e.g., adversarial prompts, noisy contexts).
Validate theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral stability to generalization under resource constraints.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of spectral stability metrics may vary across architectures and tasks.
On-the-fly SVD computation could introduce overhead, though we mitigate this with randomized SVD techniques.
Dynamic resource allocation may introduce additional complexity in implementation.
The approach assumes that spectral stability correlates with computational difficulty, which may not hold in all cases.
